📱 UX Case Study · 2026

FairShare
Budget Making App

A mobile app that makes splitting group expenses and tracking shared savings goals simple, fair and completely stress-free.

My Role
Lead UX Designer
Duration
March 2026 – May 2026
Tools
Figma · Google Forms
Type
Mobile App · UX Research
1
Project Overview
What is FairShare?

FairShare is a mobile app designed to help any age group — from college students to working professionals — split expenses, track group savings, and maintain fairness in shared financial activities. The app focuses on simplicity and clarity, making it easy for both tech-savvy youth and older users.

🎯 The Problem
Managing money in groups is confusing and creates awkward situations. People often don't know who has paid, who hasn't, and struggle to save together for shared goals without disputes.
✅ The Goal
Design a simple, clear app that splits expenses fairly, tracks payments transparently, and keeps groups motivated to save together — removing all confusion and awkwardness.
👤 My Role
Lead UX Designer responsible for user research, persona creation, wireframing, high-fidelity UI design, usability testing, design system and the final case study.
🗓️ Duration
March 2026 – Present. This is my first UX portfolio project, completed as part of the Google UX Design Certificate course.
2
User Research
Understanding the user

I surveyed 7 people across different age groups using Google Forms to understand how they currently manage shared expenses in real life. Here's what the data revealed.

7
People surveyed across different age groups
6/7
Would recommend this kind of app to friends or family
#1
Pain point — confusion and awkwardness in group expenses
1
Confusion in Tracking Payments
People often forget who has paid and who hasn't, or struggle to keep records updated — leading to disputes within the group.
2
Awkwardness in Asking for Money
Respondents feel uncomfortable reminding friends or colleagues to contribute, making expense sharing stressful and inconvenient.
3
Difficulty Splitting Large Bills
When everyone orders differently in a big group, splitting the bill becomes messy, time-consuming and often unfair.
4
Lack of Motivation for Group Saving
People lose interest or forget to save toward shared goals, and disagreements arise about how the money should be used.
"People struggle most with confusion and awkwardness when splitting group expenses — solving these is the top priority."
— Core problem statement derived from research
3
User Personas
Who we're designing for

Based on the research, I created two personas representing the primary user groups for FairShare — a working professional and a college student.

A
Aisha Khan
28 · Teacher · New York · Above average tech literacy
Goals
  • Keep group expenses transparent so everyone knows where money goes
  • Save together for shared goals like vacations or household needs
  • Encourage roommates to stay consistent and collaborative in budgeting
Frustrations
  • Gets stressed when people forget to log expenses
  • Apps that feel cluttered or complicated make her lose interest
  • Hard to track savings progress clearly, which reduces motivation
"If everyone logs their part, we'll reach our goal faster."
A
Anuv Kaur
21 · CS Student & Gamer · Bangalore · Very high tech literacy
Goals
  • Split shared expenses for gaming gear and group outings fairly
  • Save toward big goals like a new console or squad trips
  • Flexible goal customization that matches his lifestyle
Frustrations
  • Friends forgetting to log their share of expenses
  • Apps that feel boring or too formal for casual use
  • Gets annoyed when splitting uneven expenses with no flexibility
"Managing money together should feel simple, fair and stress-free."
4
Lo-Fi Wireframes
Starting the design

I mapped out the complete user flow in lo-fi Figma wireframes before adding any visual design — focusing purely on structure, logic and user experience.

Welcome + Sign In Flow
Carousel slide 1
Carousel slide 2
Carousel slide 3
Sign in
Home Screen
Empty state
With expense card
Expense Flow
Empty form
Split equally
Custom split
Expense saved ✓
Goal Creation Flow
Step 1 · Name goal
Step 2 · Amount & dates
Step 3 · Add members
Step 4 · Members added
Each person's share✓
Step 3 · Goal created! ✓
→
Key Design Decisions
Equally vs Custom Split
A toggle that auto-calculates amounts in real time. Users just check or uncheck who's included and amounts update instantly.
Why it matters
Directly solves pain point #3 — difficulty splitting large bills where everyone ordered differently. No manual maths needed.
4-Step Goal Creation
Breaking goal creation into 4 focused steps with a visible progress bar — Name → Amount → Members → Each person's share.
Why it matters
Reduces cognitive load. Users only deal with one decision at a time and always know where they are in the process.
Reminders + UPI Integration
"Send Reminder" button replaces the awkward in-person ask. UPI integration lets the person pay directly in one tap.
Why it matters
Directly solves pain point #2. 6 out of 7 research participants specifically requested reminders and UPI payment integration.
No Nav Bar on Task Screens
Navigation bar is completely hidden during expense creation and goal creation. Back arrow + screen title replaces it.
Why it matters
Keeps users focused on the task mid-flow without distractions. Same pattern used by Google Pay, Swiggy and most major apps.
5
Usability Study
Testing with real users

I conducted usability testing with participants from my original research group, asking them to complete two core tasks: add a shared expense and create a savings goal from scratch.

FindingObservationAction takenStatus
Split section unclearUsers didn't immediately understand the Equally / Custom toggle without seeing amountsAdded auto-calculated amounts next to each member name that update in real time as users toggleFixed
Currency inconsistencySome screens showed $ (USD) instead of ₹ (INR) causing confusion for Indian usersChanged all currency labels to ₹ consistently throughout the entire appFixed
Wrong subtitle on step 3Goal creation step 3 still showed "Set amount and deadline" — copied from step 2 by mistakeUpdated subtitle to "Choose who joins this goal" — correct and clearly differentFixed
Unequal split neededUsers wanted to split bills unevenly when different people ordered different amountsAdded Custom split mode with individual editable inputs and a live "₹X of ₹Y assigned" counterFixed
Goal amount undecidedSome users didn't know how much each person should save at the time of creationAdded "We will decide later" option on step 4 so the goal can be created without forcing a decisionFixed
Privacy concern1 out of 7 users expressed concern about sharing financial data in an appNoted as a limitation — future version should include granular privacy settings and data transparencyNoted

7
Accessibility
Designing for everyone

FairShare is designed to work for all age groups — from tech-savvy students to older adults who prefer straightforward, no-fuss tools.

🎨
Color contrast
All text meets WCAG AA contrast ratio requirements. Teal primary on white background tested for readability across all screen types and lighting conditions.
📝
Clear typography
Plus Jakarta Sans at minimum 14px body size. Bold weights used consistently for key amounts, names and headings throughout the app.
🔘
Large tap targets
All interactive elements are minimum 44px height — following Apple HIG and Google Material Design accessibility guidelines for mobile touch targets.
💬
Plain language
Copy is written conversationally — "Who's saving with you?" instead of "Add members". Makes the app approachable for all ages and backgrounds.
🚦
Status indicators
Color is never the only indicator — badges always include text labels like "pending", "settled", "added" alongside color so colorblind users can follow.
📍
Progress steppers
All multi-step flows show a visual progress bar and step count — "Step 2 of 4" — so users always know where they are and how much is left.
8
Takeaways
What I learned

This was my first complete UX portfolio project — from research to hi-fi prototype to design system. Here's what the process taught me.

Impact
During usability testing, participants responded very positively to the reminder feature — saying it would save them awkward money conversations with friends every month. The "Send Reminder" button was called out as the most useful feature. 6 out of 7 research participants said they would recommend such an app to their friends or family, which validates the core problem being solved.
What I learned
Research reveals things you would never assume. I thought the main problem was calculating splits — but users told me the real pain was the awkwardness of asking for money back. Without talking to users first, I would have built the completely wrong solution. I also learned that a design system saves enormous time during hi-fi — having color variables and reusable components made iteration much faster. Most importantly, I learned to trust my own instincts — several design decisions I made myself turned out to be exactly right.
9
Next Steps
Where FairShare goes next

If I were to continue developing FairShare beyond this case study, here are the three things I would prioritize next.

01
Real UPI Integration
Implement actual GPay, PhonePe and Paytm deep links so payments happen inside the app with one tap — not just a prototype simulation.
02
Second usability study
Run a second round of testing on the hi-fi prototype with new participants to validate all the design changes made after round 1.
03
Privacy settings
One research participant raised concerns about sharing financial data. Add granular privacy controls and clear data transparency to address this.
